title: M/s Infosys BPM Limited v. Ramesh Kumar Sinha
court: High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
keyArguments: The respondent employee was found to have committed gross misconduct by leaking confidential client data to a competitor firm, which constituted a fundamental breach of the employment contract and the company's Code of Conduct.

The domestic enquiry conducted by the petitioner company was fair and in accordance with the principles of natural justice, as the respondent was given adequate notice, opportunity to cross-examine witnesses, and to present his defense.

The Labour Court erred in law by substituting its own judgment on the quantum of punishment, as the misconduct was proven and the employer's discretion in imposing dismissal was reasonable and proportionate.

The respondent's contention of victimization was unsubstantiated by any documentary or oral evidence, and the enquiry officer's findings were based on cogent material including digital forensic reports.

Under Section 11A of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, the Labour Court's power to interfere with the punishment of dismissal is limited where the misconduct is proved and the punishment is not shockingly disproportionate.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Karnataka set aside the Labour Court's award which had reinstated the respondent-workman Ramesh Kumar Sinha with back wages. The High Court held that the domestic enquiry was conducted fairly and the findings of misconduct were supported by substantial evidence including a digital forensic audit report. The court ruled that the Labour Court exceeded its jurisdiction by interfering with the punishment of dismissal, which was proportionate to the gravity of the misconduct involving breach of confidentiality and data theft. The writ petition filed by the employer was allowed.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU

WRIT PETITION NO. 14872 OF 2022

BETWEEN:
M/s Infosys BPM Limited, represented by its Authorized Signatory, Bengaluru ... Petitioner

AND:
Ramesh Kumar Sinha, S/o Late Mohan Kumar Sinha, Residing at No. 34, 5th Cross, Rajajinagar, Bengaluru - 560010 ... Respondent

This writ petition is filed under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India praying to quash the award dated 15.03.2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court No. II, Bengaluru in Reference No. KA/LC-II/CR-44/2019.

Hearing concluded on 18.07.2023. Judgment pronounced on 04.09.2023.

JUDGMENT

The petitioner-company is engaged in business process management services and employs a large workforce handling sensitive client data. The respondent was employed as a Senior Process Associate since 2014. In January 2019, a client of the petitioner company reported suspected leakage of proprietary business data. A digital forensic investigation conducted by an independent agency revealed that the respondent had transmitted confidential client files to an external email address linked to a competitor entity. Consequently, a charge sheet was issued and a domestic enquiry was conducted.

The Enquiry Officer, after affording full opportunity to the respondent including the right to cross-examine witnesses and peruse documentary evidence, returned a finding of proved misconduct. Based on these findings, the disciplinary authority dismissed the respondent from service vide order dated 22.05.2019. The respondent raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the Labour Court. The Labour Court, by the impugned award, held that while misconduct was proved, the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate and directed reinstatement with fifty percent back wages.

This Court has carefully examined the record of the domestic enquiry, the award of the Labour Court, and the submissions of learned counsel for both parties.

On the question of fairness of the domestic enquiry, this Court finds that the respondent was given due notice, the charges were clearly communicated, he was permitted to engage a co-worker as his defence representative, and he had the opportunity to examine and cross-examine witnesses. The principles of natural justice were duly observed. The Labour Court itself did not find the enquiry to be vitiated, and this finding is affirmed.

On the question of proof of misconduct, the digital forensic report conclusively established that confidential client data was transmitted from the respondent's official workstation using his authenticated credentials to an external domain. The respondent offered no satisfactory explanation for this act. The Enquiry Officer's conclusion was based on reliable and cogent material. This Court finds no perversity in the findings.

The critical question is whether the Labour Court was justified in interfering with the punishment of dismissal. The Supreme Court in Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate (2005) 2 SCC 489 and in BHEL v. Suresh Kumar Panda has consistently held that where misconduct is proved, courts should be slow to interfere with the punishment imposed by the employer unless it is so disproportionate as to shock the conscience of the court. Leakage of client data in the information technology and BPM sector strikes at the very root of the employment relationship and the employer's obligation to its clients. The misconduct herein is of the gravest nature. The punishment of dismissal cannot be said to be disproportionate in these circumstances.

The Labour Court committed a jurisdictional error by substituting its own view on the adequacy of punishment without any legal basis warranting interference. The award dated 15.03.2022 is hereby quashed and set aside. The writ petition is allowed. No order as to costs.

Sd/-
HONOURBLE MR. JUSTICE PRAKASH S. NAIR
High Court of Karnataka
